## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, today I want to talk about the creative process and how you can get your work to be made more easily, be more powerful, and be more like you. My name is Nicholas Wilton, I'm a fine artist. We're standing in my studio in Sausalito. I've been painting my entire life, making art my whole life. I've worked with thousands of people helping them bring their work out to make their work more potent and more powerful and more like themselves. This happened quite by accident, actually happened through my own struggle early on. I was always, always trying to make work and I struggled frankly. I struggled a lot trying to make my work powerful and actually just make work that I liked. I know a lot of you can relate to this, you know, on one day you make something that's really great and sometimes that can be done so easily and then you'll work two more days and then you make a few more things and those are terrible and then you feel like giving up and then you get frustrated and then you make something else and then that looks really great and then you do something you think you hate and then two weeks later you like it. There is such, it's so confusing and it's so difficult and I went to an amazing art school, I went to Art Center College of Design and I mean I aced it there and I had incredible teachers and I've worked really hard and I've been able to thrive as an artist and I'm thankful for that and I love it, I love my life, I love the creative process but it really, it really didn't get going, it really didn't happen for me until I started to really pay attention to the process of my work and what was actually happening. I started to take notes basically when things were working and when things weren't working. Over a decade, several twenty years of writing down when, why it was working, how did this happen, how could I be so good on Wednesday and so bad on Thursday and what came out of this was a system, was a set of principles and I call them the art life principles and I've been teaching these now for about twenty years and this has evolved over time through a lot of trial and error but I've distilled it down so these ideas once understood, now it's not just like ABC, there's some complexity here but if you can get these principles, if you can understand these principles, you can transform your work. What I am amazed and when I first started teaching this twenty years ago that I could take somebody and who was vaguely, minorly interested in art, had maybe no experience at all and given these principles, they could actually make work that starts feeling incredibly powerful and looks and feels like their own work. So I'm not interested in teaching a set of techniques or teaching a way of working, you know, certain kind of materials. I'm talking about overarching principles, these ideas that

## [03:04 - 06:08]

are bigger than the materials. So this is a set of principles, I call them the art life principles and these I've used to help firstly myself streamline my process and allow myself to make work that I love and that is able to, I've been able to sell this work, I'm able to do gallery shows or license my work or whatever I want to do with it but mostly I can please myself that I love what I'm doing and that I can, there's a room in it for me to improve and most importantly that it feels like my own, that it really feels like I'm making work that I love and that's the offer, that's the possibility that there's a transformation that you can have with your work and it's not about spending, you know, nine months in front of a model drawing to improve and we all did this, you know, practice and practice and practice and practice and at a certain point even in your life you realize like my god, this is just taking forever and it's really hard, it's really difficult and you know, I really hone this down so these principles can give you, you know, can speed up this process so you don't actually even have to be painting that much, it's just an awareness, you got to start becoming aware of these ideas and these principles and your work will transform, that's the power of the art life principles. There's six of these, design, value, color, risk, texture and soul. Some of these are pretty, we've all heard of them before but I've arranged them in a hierarchical order, that's what makes them very powerful, some things are more powerful than others and we need to understand how they relate and why so we can better utilize them. I want to start right now with the very first principle, design, art life principle number one, it's the most powerful and this has the possibility to really help you see your work in a new way so let's get going. Okay so I've written out the six art life principles here, design, value, color, texture, risk and soul. We're going to be diving into in this series of videos upcoming, the first three of these. ## [06:08 - 09:09]

What I'm showing you, what I'm sharing with you is really powerful and I was never really shown this, we just have all these different parts of art making but I was never given anything in an order of their relative strength and I think that's something that you really want to start thinking about. If we're talking about design, I first want to talk about differences because differences are something that is in the entire, in all my teaching, I sort of use that as the comeback to place of how to make your work more stronger and how to kind of troubleshoot. If we're going, we're doing the same thing every day, let's say we're going to a job and it's the same job and we go there day in and day out for six months straight and then we go on vacation somewhere, that vacation is really extraordinary because of what came before, it's really different than what we've experienced. Your birthday comes once a year, that's sometimes a pretty amazing day, it's certainly a different day because you only get one of them a year. Walking down the road and you bump into a friend that you haven't seen for a long time, that's an amazing event, it really wakes you up. Things that come along in your life that are different than what's happened before make you feel alive and I think that's a really, really important thing to get that people buy art because they can't make it and they see it and they are moved by it mostly because it's something that they haven't experienced before and it feels really different and so they're just drawn to it and they want it. The reason I'm kind of describing this feeling is that we want to have this feeling when we're making our work. Feeling alive happens when we're doing something that we haven't kind of done before. So if we're making a piece of art and it's the same thing and we're doing the same thing over and over and over again, I know a lot of you have experienced this, you kind of end up going into a rut and it just starts boring, you put off going into the studio but once to sort of break free of that you usually have to kind of start doing something new and something kind of different. So this idea of differences in your life and in your art is what creates powerful art and it kind of threads its way into design which I want to talk to now. So design and I know, look, we've all, everyone knows about design, it's not something that's new but kind of thinking it in this way

## [09:09 - 12:17]

is a slightly different twist. Design, the way I just find it, is just simply the arrangement of shapes on a picture. So if I have a picture, now this could be a photograph or it could be a sculpture or whatever, it doesn't matter but I'll say picture because that's kind of where I'm starting from. If I have a picture and there's a mark on it and then I have another mark down here and I have something over here, this is the design. It's the arrangement of whatever shapes you get. Now I've made abstract little shapes here but this could be a picture of something, this could be a dog, this could be a telephone pole, this could be a forest, it doesn't really matter what kind of work you do. It could be a photograph, it could be a realistic, you know, photograph of a person but when you can clearly see the shapes of things and you can look at in some total, that's the design of the work. We're interested in making design really, really strong, strong design and strong design comes from differences. You know, differences that we can see, so a really obvious one is scale, different sizes, different shapes. There's, you know, so if I take this and I make this a little circle and I make this much bigger, so I'm making this, I'm making this different than this in several ways. This is a square, so it's kind of hard edged and this is a circle and it's kind of round. This is also much bigger and what happens when we look at a picture or a sculpture or a photograph or anything in our world, we look at part of it and then instantly within a millisecond we look at the other part, we go to the next noticeable thing and when we see it, it registers whether it's different or not. So if we start here and we go all the way down here, when this is different, it wakes us up. That's what makes it kind of come alive for us. So if I want to add something else here, if I added a little square here and we go from here over to this square, it's a square but it's also kind of the same size and it's kind of feels the same.
